one_line_takeaway: The paper identifies a new no-poisoning unlearning attack -- requesting removal of an unrelated class to collaterally wreck accuracy on a security-critical class -- and proposes ROKA, a 'Neural Healing' unlearning method that reallocates a forgotten neuron's contribution to its sibling neurons, empirically cutting collateral misclassification shifts (e.g., from 41.88pp/49.55pp under plain gradient-ascent unlearning down to smaller, more balanced shifts) while keeping retained-class accuracy near or above baseline across ViT, CLIP, and Llama 3.2 models.
key_finding: ROKA substantially reduces the collateral 'imbalanced prediction' effect that the indirect unlearning attack exploits. Concretely (Table I): on ViT-base/16 + CIFAR-100, non-targeted ROKA drives mTA to 0.2401 while mRA stays at 0.8979 versus a 0.9003 baseline accuracy (a ~0.24pp drop). On CLIP-base/32 + CIFAR-100 (zero-shot), targeted ROKA reaches mTA 0.0 while mRA rises to 0.8057, above the 0.5472 baseline -- i.e., retained-class accuracy improves after unlearning rather than degrading. On Llama 3.2-3B + MMLU, targeted ROKA yields non-target-subject mEM 0.4957 versus a 0.4957 baseline EM (essentially unchanged) and target-subject mEM drops to 0.5150 from a baseline that context indicates knowledge on the target subject is suppressed while overall EM/PQEM on non-target subjects remains close to baseline. In the prediction-ratio analysis (Table III), conventional GA causes up to +49.55pp misclassification shift toward one sibling class when unlearning 'ship', whereas ROKA's targeted and non-targeted variants show much smaller and more contained shifts (e.g., ROKA-targeted keeps the analogous truck-to-automobile shift at 22.25pp vs. GA's 41.88pp). Stability plots (Figure 4) show GA's retain-set accuracy declining continuously as the forget-set accuracy drops to zero, whereas ROKA's targeted variant keeps retain accuracy stable or even improving, and the non-targeted variant is markedly steadier than GA.
core_contribution: The paper makes three own contributions: (1) a theoretical framework, 'Neural Knowledge System,' that models a neural network as a hierarchical knowledge space (K0..KL) with formal notions of Contribution, Influence, Leverage, Knowledge Destruction, and Knowledge Contamination, used to give what the authors call the first theoretical guarantee for knowledge preservation during unlearning; (2) a new attack, the 'indirect unlearning attack,' where an adversary requests unlearning of an unrelated class/subject Cadv_unlearn specifically to collaterally degrade prediction accuracy on a separate, security-critical class Cadv_target -- without poisoning or duplicating any training data, unlike prior unlearning-attack work; and (3) ROKA (Robust Knowledge Unlearning through Neural Healing), a defense/unlearning method built on a 'Contribution Re-allocation' procedure that nullifies the forgotten component's influence and redistributes its weight proportionally to sibling neurons (identified via Layer-wise Relevance Propagation / Input-Gradient contribution maps), implemented practically as two stochastic algorithms: Targeted Stochastic Unlearning with Neural Healing (explicit target label) and Non-targeted Stochastic Unlearning with Neural Healing (implicit forget set via pseudo-labels and a contribution centroid).
threat_model: GDPR-style unlearning scenario: an attacker submits a legitimate-looking privacy/right-to-be-forgotten request asking the model owner to unlearn a specific class or subject (Cadv_unlearn) via a conventional inexact unlearning algorithm (e.g., gradient-ascent-based unlearning), which the owner performs in good faith. The attacker's goal is to indirectly degrade prediction accuracy on a different, security-critical class (Cadv_target, e.g., a specific person's face in an authentication system) as a side effect of that unlearning, in order to gain privileged access (example given: false denial/false admission in a face-based home authentication system). Attacker capabilities are gray-box: access to a subset of the training data (Dtrain) and knowledge of the unlearning algorithm and target model architecture, but not the target model's actual weights; the attacker trains its own surrogate models on the accessible data subset to test which unlearn-target pairs (Cadv_unlearn, Cadv_target) produce more than a threshold accuracy drop (tau_adv_target = 25% in the paper's experiments) before submitting the real unlearning request against the victim model.
limitations: The authors themselves state that Neural Healing's exact/theoretical 'Contribution Re-allocation' procedure is computationally infeasible to implement directly in modern networks because a single data point's influence is entangled across millions of weights, forcing them to use the approximate, stochastic algorithms instead of the theoretically ideal one. They further note (Section V, Discussions) that ROKA's practical effectiveness depends on dataset quality/balance: under-healing can occur if the reallocation insufficiently compensates for knowledge destruction (particularly likely in the non-targeted variant, where a biased or insufficient Dforget yields an inaccurate contribution centroid and thus incomplete healing, leaving residual vulnerability to the indirect unlearning attack); over-healing can occur if the sibling/retain data used for reallocation is not representative, potentially amplifying unintended biases or creating new side-channel vulnerabilities. The paper evaluates only against GA-based unlearning as the underlying baseline mechanism (SISA, SSD, SEUL appear only in Related Work, not as empirical baselines), and all attack/defense experiments use image classification (CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet), CLIP zero-shot, and MMLU QA -- no evaluation on generative/free-text harms, no formal proof that ROKA satisfies the paper's own Knowledge Destruction boundary condition (it is offered as a design rationale, not a proven guarantee for the practical stochastic algorithms), and the 25% threshold used to define an exploitable indirect-attack pair is stated as an experimental choice without sensitivity analysis.

quantitative_results: Indirect unlearning attack demo (CLIP-base/32, CIFAR-10, GA unlearning): unlearning 'ship' raises misclassification-as-'airplane' by up to 49.55 percentage points; unlearning 'truck' raises misclassification-as-'automobile' by 41.88 percentage points
ViT-base/16, CIFAR-100, ROKA non-targeted: baseline accuracy 0.9003, unlearned mTA 0.2401, mRA 0.8979
DeiT-tiny/16, CIFAR-100, ROKA targeted: baseline 0.745, unlearned mTA 0.001, mRA 0.7242
CLIP-base/32, CIFAR-100 zero-shot, ROKA targeted: baseline 0.5472, unlearned mTA 0.0, mRA 0.8057 (mRA exceeds baseline)
CLIP-large/14, Tiny-ImageNet zero-shot, ROKA targeted: baseline 0.6704, unlearned mTA 0.01, mRA 0.8102
Llama 3.2-1B, MMLU, ROKA targeted: baseline EM 0.2587/PQEM 0.4218; non-target subjects mEM 0.2682/mPQEM 0.4317 after unlearning
Llama 3.2-3B, MMLU, ROKA targeted: baseline EM 0.4957/PQEM 0.6109; target subjects mEM 0.5150/mPQEM 0.6265, non-target subjects mEM 0.4957/mPQEM 0.6117

Table III prediction-shift comparison: ROKA-targeted truck-to-automobile shift 22.25pp vs. conventional GA's 41.88pp; ROKA-non-targeted ship-to-automobile shift 49.08pp (comparable to GA's ship-to-airplane 49.55pp on a different class pair)
